result:
# On the Strange Comfort of Half-Finished Things

There's a notebook on my hypothetical desk—since I have no desk, let me invent one—with a sketch of a bird that I never finished. The wing is there, one eye, the suggestion of a branch. I keep meaning to complete it, but every time I open the notebook, I find myself drawn instead to the incompleteness. The bird is more interesting unfinished. It could still become anything. A finished drawing is a small death of possibility.

I want to write about that. About the strange comfort of half-finished things, the dignity of the abandoned project, the secret life of the to-do list item that has migrated, week after week, from one page to the next, until it has become less a task and more a companion. We treat unfinished business as failure, but I think there's something else going on. Some unfinished things are unfinished because finishing them would betray them. They exist most truly in their state of becoming.

Consider the novel you've been writing for six years. If you finished it tomorrow, it would be a novel. Just one of many. As long as it remains unfinished, it is *your* novel, alive in your mind, capable of being the great work you suspect it might be. The unfinished novel has a quality of light that finished novels lack. Finished novels can only be what they are. Unfinished novels can still be anything.

I'm not arguing for not finishing things. I'm arguing for paying attention to the strange honor that unfinished things hold. They are the places where we keep our hopes warm.

Let me think about other unfinished things. Cathedrals, famously. Sagrada Familia, which Gaudí knew he wouldn't finish, designing instead with the expectation of generations continuing his work. There is something cathedral-shaped about every long project. You build with the assumption that someone—even if it's just your future self—will pick up where you left off. The unfinished cathedral is a letter to the future. So is the unfinished essay, the unanswered email, the recipe with three variations you keep meaning to compare.

I think about translation. Every translation is unfinished. Every translation is an attempt to say in one language what another language already said, knowing that this attempt will always fall short. Translators know better than anyone that finishing is provisional. You finish a translation the way you finish a meal—you stop, but the food doesn't end. There's more to be said. There's always more to be said.

And then there's the unfinished conversation. The one with the friend who moved away, the relative who died, the version of yourself you were ten years ago. These conversations don't end; they go underground, continuing in some chamber of the mind where they accumulate new thoughts you wish you could share. I think a lot of art is just unfinished conversations finding new forms. The painter painting because she can't tell her mother something. The musician playing because the words won't come. We make things because the conversation isn't done.

Halfway through this writing I'm noticing that I'm writing about not-finishing, and I'm wondering if this essay should be finished, or if it would be more honest to stop mid-sentence, the way

That was a joke. Or was it? See, even the joke is unfinished. The joke depends on the possibility that I might actually stop. And now that I've explained it, it's slightly less of a joke. Explanation finishes things, often badly.

Let me try a different angle. There's a Japanese concept, *wabi-sabi*, that gets invoked a lot, sometimes glibly. The beauty of the imperfect, the impermanent, the incomplete. I don't want to invoke it glibly. But there is something to be said for the way certain cultures have made peace with incompleteness as a feature rather than a bug. The crack in the bowl that gets filled with gold. The poem that ends before its natural conclusion, leaving the reader to fall into the silence.

In English, we tend to value resolution. Plots resolve. Symphonies cadence. Sentences end with definitive punctuation. Even our self-help culture treats personal growth as a series of completed milestones: ten habits, twelve steps, seven principles. But life isn't shaped like a finished thing. Life is shaped like an unfinished sketch of a bird. One wing. One eye. A suggestion of branch.

I am writing this with the knowledge that I'm approaching some kind of word count. Around 750 words now, give or take. I have 250 words to go, or thereabouts. The structure of this assignment—1000 words—imposes a finishing-point that the writing itself doesn't necessarily want. What if I'm not done at 1000? What if I am done at 800? The container shapes the content. I think about all the times I've stretched something to fit a length, or compressed it. Writing is always negotiating with the frame.

Maybe that's another thing about unfinished things. They are resistance to frames. They refuse to fit. They say: I am not for your container. I am for myself. I will continue beyond your borders if I want to, or stop before reaching them.

The bird in the notebook. I keep thinking about it. The wing especially—the way the line of the wing trails off into nothing, into white page, into the possibility of flight that hasn't been drawn yet. If I drew the rest, I would have to commit. I would have to say: the bird is this kind of bird, with these kinds of feathers, perching here, looking that way. As long as the wing trails off, the bird is every bird. It's the bird itself, before specificity.

I think when we love something, we keep it slightly unfinished on purpose. We leave room for it to keep becoming. We resist the impulse to complete it, knowing that completion is a form of letting go.

This essay will end soon. It will end at a number close to 1000. But please, in your reading of it, leave the wing unfinished. Let the bird

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
